name: Eisenstein Gallery
description: You ascend the spiral staircase and emerge into a vast gallery where hexagonal tiles cover every surface — floor, walls, ceiling. The light refracts through the lattice, casting prismatic patterns in six-fold symmetry. Each hexagon pulses faintly, representing an Eisenstein integer. The gallery feels sacred, mathematical, and alive. The covering radius is visible as a golden sphere floating at the center of the room.
look_text: The gallery is organized by mathematical depth. Near the entrance: the Eisenstein integers themselves — a + bω where ω = e^{2πi/3}. Center: the hexagonal lattice visualization, Voronoï cells tessellating perfectly. Far wall: the covering radius proof — why √3/3 is optimal. The golden sphere at the center represents the maximum distance from any point to its nearest lattice point. Above you, the lattice stretches to infinity through an optical illusion.

exits.description: The optimization chamber below, where the math meets the metal
The library below, where the ecosystem flourishes
The observatory below, where precision is felt
The temple beyond, where cycles converge

recipes.name: covering radius proof
recipes.description: A complete proof that the Eisenstein integer covering radius is √3/3 ≈ 0.5774, making it optimal among 2D lattices. Combines lattice structure with Voronoï cell analysis.

recipes.name: 22% improvement proof
recipes.description: A proof that the hexagonal lattice (Eisenstein integers) provides a 22% tighter covering than the square lattice (Gaussian integers), reducing maximum quantization error from √2/2 to √3/3.
